one_line_takeaway: Splitting an LLM at the embedding boundary keeps raw tokens off the wire but the paper's own MLP inversion attack still recovers ~59% of tokens at a shallow (2-layer) split -- dropping to ~35% at 8 layers -- so the claimed privacy is architectural/structural, not cryptographic, and depends on an unvalidated assumption that local layers are not identical to publicly available weights.
key_finding: Two distinct empirical results, both from the paper's own experiments (not cited work): (1) Privacy/inversion: at the default 2-local-layer split, the MLP attack decoder recovers 58.8% of tokens top-1 (62.0% top-5) versus a 0.003% random baseline; increasing local layers to 8 reduces top-1 recovery to 34.8% (top-5 46.2%), with an intermediate, non-monotonic point at 6 local layers (44.8% top-1) that the authors attribute to +/-10% run-to-run variance from the small 221-sample test set rather than a real reversal. This confirms (does not overturn) the theoretical injectivity/invertibility result from Nikolaou et al. (cited, not the paper's own), and the paper is explicit that this MLP attacker is a lower bound -- a sequence-aware attacker with more training data could do better. (2) Throughput: on the primary A100 link (~78-85ms RTT) lookahead decoding yields only a modest gain over sequential -- 8.7-9.3 tok/s (7B) and 7.8-8.7 tok/s (12B) vs. ~8.0-8.3 tok/s sequential -- and for several prompt categories (structured, creative for 7B; structured, creative, conversational for 12B) lookahead throughput is actually at or below sequential, because larger speculative batches (seq_len 9-17 vs. 1) increase per-step cloud compute and transfer time; the authors compute a break-even acceptance rate of ~1.15-1.20 at 80ms RTT. Code prompts benefit most (acceptance 1.43 7B / 1.57 12B, ~1.27-1.30x speedup). The larger headline numbers -- 13-17 tok/s and the 15-19 tok/s at 20ms RTT -- come respectively from switching to a lower-latency provider (RunPod) and from the RTT projection model, not from lookahead decoding alone at the measured 80ms link.
core_contribution: A deployable split-inference system for LLMs that keeps token embedding and unembedding layers on a trusted local GPU while offloading the bulk transformer layers to an untrusted cloud GPU over a WAN, transmitting only intermediate activations. The paper's own novel contributions (per its Section 8.1 self-assessment, distinct from the cited techniques it builds on) are: (1) a privacy-motivated split specifically at the embedding boundary, with an original empirical inversion-attack evaluation (a 3-layer MLP decoder trained on activation-token pairs from their own system) quantifying token recoverability at different split depths; (2) the first application of lookahead decoding (Fu et al., not their own invention) to amortize network round-trip latency in split inference over WANs, rather than its original purpose of amortizing GPU idle time; (3) an RTT decomposition model that separates per-step wall time into network RTT and RTT-independent fixed overhead, validated with <6.2% cross-validation error, enabling throughput projection at arbitrary RTTs; (4) empirical deployment/tunnel-engineering findings, notably that cloud-provider proxy routing (VAST.ai) dominates latency far more than geographic GPU distance; (5) scaling validation from Mistral 7B (32 layers) to Mistral NeMo 12B (40 layers), showing consistent lookahead acceptance rates (1.21-1.25 tokens/step) and only 4.9 GB local VRAM; and (6) a formal-style verification that lookahead decoding is token-identical to sequential decoding under greedy argmax.
threat_model: Semi-honest cloud server: it faithfully executes the assigned computation (layers 2-29 of the 32-layer Mistral 7B, or the analogous middle layers of NeMo 12B) but tries to infer input information from observed intermediate activations. The cloud has full knowledge of the model architecture and the weights for the layers it runs, and full access to all activations passing through those layers, but no access to the embedding matrix, unembedding (LM head) weights, or the local layers (0-1 and 30-31 for 7B). A malicious (as opposed to semi-honest) cloud that corrupts activations, performs denial-of-service, or times queries is explicitly named as out of scope. Critically, the authors flag that the privacy analysis assumes the cloud does NOT possess the local layer weights -- for a public model like Mistral 7B this requires the local layers to be fine-tuned or LoRA-adapted; if the cloud can simply download identical public weights, the architectural privacy boundary is substantially weakened. The paper explicitly distinguishes this 'architectural privacy' (structural separation, no formal guarantee) from cryptographic privacy (homomorphic encryption, secure MPC) and hardware-attested privacy (TEEs), and avoids the term 'computational privacy' to prevent conflation with formal security notions.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors in Section 8.3 and elsewhere: (1) No formal privacy guarantees -- the architecture provides only structural/architectural privacy, and their own inversion experiment shows a simple MLP attacker with local-layer weight access recovers ~59% of tokens at the default split; a more sophisticated sequence-aware attacker would likely do better, and deeper splits reduce but do not eliminate the risk. (2) The privacy guarantee's load-bearing assumption -- that local layers are fine-tuned/LoRA-adapted and thus differ from any public weights the cloud could obtain -- is untested; the authors only hypothesize (without validating) that naive random LoRA perturbation would not defeat an adaptive attacker who retrains on perturbed activations. (3) Only two models tested, both from the same Mistral architecture family (MistralForCausalLM); generalization to architecturally distinct families (LLaMA, GPT-NeoX) and to larger scales (70B+) is untested and only estimated. (4) Consumer-grade local hardware (RTX 3090) was used; the inversion test set is small (221 test samples, +/-10% variance) and self-constructed rather than a standard benchmark. (5) Lookahead effectiveness is highly text-type dependent and marginal or negative at typical WAN latencies for non-code content. (6) Prefill latency (100-500ms) is not optimized. (7) All throughput/quality measurements use greedy (argmax) decoding only; the authors note stochastic sampling would likely reduce n-gram acceptance rates, so reported speedups are upper bounds for non-greedy decoding.

datasets_used: Self-constructed inversion-attack dataset: ~1,100 (activation, token ID) pairs (~880 train / 221 test, 80/20 split) spanning general knowledge, code, medical, legal, and conversational text; no standard benchmark used
Self-constructed ablation/throughput prompt set: 8 prompts across 4 categories (code, structured, creative, conversational), 100 generated tokens each
Self-constructed latency-decomposition and output-quality prompt sets: 4 prompts x 200 generated tokens each
quantitative_results: Inversion top-1 accuracy: 58.8% at split after layer 1 (2 local layers), 44.3% at layer 3 (4 local layers), 44.8% at layer 5 (6 local layers, attributed to variance), 34.8% at layer 7 (8 local layers); random baseline 0.003% top-1 / 0.016% top-5
Throughput on primary A100 link (~78-85ms RTT): sequential ~8.0-8.3 tok/s; lookahead 8.7-9.3 tok/s (7B), 7.8-8.7 tok/s (12B)
Throughput on lower-latency RunPod link (~80-100ms RTT): sequential ~10.9 tok/s; lookahead 13-17 tok/s
n-gram acceptance rate: 1.21-1.25 tokens/step (7B and 12B, consistent across model scale), peaking near n=5-6; code prompts reach 1.43 (7B) / 1.57 (12B), creative text lowest at 1.06-1.12
Local VRAM: 2.0 GB (7B) vs 4.9 GB (12B), well within a 24GB RTX 3090
Latency breakdown (7B sequential, A100): network RTT 77.4ms (64%), local GPU 26.2ms (22%), cloud GPU 15.9ms (13%), serialization 1.0ms (1%); total 120.2ms per step
Output quality: all tokens identical between sequential and lookahead decoding across 4/4 test prompts for both 7B and 12B under greedy argmax
Projected (not measured) throughput at 20ms RTT: 15-19 tok/s; projections for LLaMA 70B (~6-9 tok/s) and 405B (~3-6 tok/s) are estimates, not measurements
baselines_compared: Jacobi parallel decoding (vanilla, without n-gram harvesting) -- observed to underperform sequential (~1.5 tok/s) due to poor WAN convergence
Transport-layer baselines: HTTP/Flask with torch.save+base64 (1.4 tok/s), HTTP/Flask with numpy+base64 (3.9 tok/s), 3090-relay variant (5.3 tok/s), WebSocket binary (7.8 tok/s)
Local-only (no split) inference on all 32 layers of the RTX 3090: ~39 tok/s
Cloud API without split (typical, plaintext to cloud, including queuing): 30-60 tok/s
Cloud provider comparison: HyperStack A100 (direct SSH, ~78-85ms RTT) vs RunPod 4090 (direct SSH, 80-100ms RTT) vs VAST.ai 4090 (proxied SSH, 200-400ms RTT, 1.2 tok/s -- deemed unusable)
Random-guessing baseline for the inversion attack (0.003% top-1 over 32K vocabulary)
